serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of braised greens with chestnuts is approximately 280 g, comprising braised baby cabbage, whole or halved chestnuts, and a savoury thickened broth.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: USDA FoodData Central — Chestnuts (roasted), Bok choy / Chinese cabbage (cooked, boiled), and standard Chinese braised vegetable dish parameters; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for cooked Chinese vegetables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 栗子扒菜胆 (Braised Greens with Chestnuts) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 55% braised Chinese leafy greens (cai dan / baby cabbage), 25% chestnuts (roasted), and 20% savoury braising broth with oyster sauce and cornstarch thickener. USDA FDC reference items used: Chestnuts, Chinese, roasted (FDC ID 170578); Cabbage, Chinese (pak-choi), cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169975); and a standard oyster-sauce-based braising liquid. Saffron contributes negligible macronutrients at culinary quantities. Values represent a plausible per-100g estimate for the combined dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chestnuts and cooked Chinese leafy vegetables — analogous estimate based on: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for chestnuts and cooked vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 栗子扒菜胆 as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: chestnuts have a published GI of approximately 54 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008), and cooked Chinese leafy greens have a negligible GI contribution. The braising broth with cornstarch thickener adds a modest glycemic contribution. Weighted composite GI estimated at 45 (low category). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 36.96) / 100 ≈ 16.6, where carbohydrate per 280g serving = 13.2 × 2.8 = 36.96g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from oyster sauce and braising broth
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 310 mg sodium per 100g (approximately 868 mg per restaurant serving), individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sauce preparation, as sodium intake from this dish can represent a substantial fraction of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by chestnut starch and high vegetable fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 45 (low) and moderate glycemic load of approximately 16.6 per serving, braised greens with chestnuts is a suitable vegetable-forward dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the cornstarch-thickened sauce should be accounted for in total carbohydrate budgeting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high vegetable content, and beneficial micronutrients including potassium and folate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat (approximately 0.3g per 100g) and provides cardioprotective micronutrients including potassium (280 mg/100g), folate, and vitamin C from the leafy greens; however, the moderate sodium content from the braising sauce warrants attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease managing blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Greens with Chestnuts (栗子扒菜胆, Lì Zǐ Pá Cài Dǎn) is a classic Chinese vegetable dish featuring tender baby cabbage or cai dan (菜胆) slow-braised in a rich, savoury broth alongside whole or halved chestnuts. The dish is finished with a glossy, lightly thickened sauce made from oyster sauce, garlic, ginger, sesame oil, and cornstarch, and is characteristically coloured and flavoured with a small quantity of saffron, which imparts a golden hue and subtle floral aroma. The texture contrasts soft, silky braised greens with the mealy, slightly sweet flesh of the chestnuts. Nutritionally, the dish is relatively low in calories (approximately 72 kcal per 100g) and fat, with a moderate carbohydrate content primarily from chestnut starch. It provides useful amounts of vitamin A, vitamin C, folate, calcium, and potassium from the leafy greens. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making it appropriate for glycemic management. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from the oyster-sauce-based braising liquid, which may reach approximately 868 mg per restaurant serving. The dish is suitable for vegetable-forward eating patterns and is commonly served as a shared side dish in Chinese banquet or restaurant settings in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. It is halal-compatible when prepared with halal-certified oyster sauce.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 栗子扒菜胆 (Braised Greens with Chestnuts) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component analysis of the identified main ingredients using USDA FoodData Central reference entries for roasted chestnuts and cooked Chinese leafy greens, with a standard oyster-sauce braising liquid contribution. GI is an analogous estimate based on published chestnut GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated (source_match_type: estimated) as no direct food composition entry was found for this specific dish in any approved primary source. Values should be treated as indicative and updated if an exact HPB Singapore Foodo or equivalent entry becomes available. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no trans fats expected in this plant-based braised dish with no hydrogenated fats). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — dish contains no animal flesh; oyster sauce contributes negligible cholesterol at culinary quantities). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — dish is plant-based with no significant B12 sources). 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin D-containing ingredients present). 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC component data and are not individually confirmed by a single approved source entry. 7. Saffron is listed in the dish description; at culinary quantities (typically <0.1g per serving) it contributes negligible macronutrients and has not been separately accounted for in the nutrient estimate. 8. The dish is served at See Ya Tonight 今晚有局 (Singapore) at SGD 33.80, consistent with a premium restaurant preparation. 9. Halal status is conditional on use of halal-certified oyster sauce; the dish contains no pork or alcohol by default preparation.
